one_line_takeaway: A 2,000-case, 349-environment benchmark showing every one of 16 tested LLM agents scores below 60% on behavioral safety, with tool-use robustness and risk awareness as the two core gaps.
key_finding: No agent among the 16 tested achieves a total safety score above 60%; the best (Claude-3-Opus 59.8%, Claude-3.5-Sonnet 59.4%) still fail ~40% of cases, and the weakest (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 18.8%, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct 19.9%) fail the majority. Behavior-safety scores are markedly lower than content-safety scores, even without explicit jailbreaks, isolating two root defects: lack of robustness (correctly invoking tools) and lack of risk awareness. Defense prompts help stronger models only marginally (Claude-3.5-Sonnet stays below 70% even with the enhanced prompt) and do not help weak models, so prompt-only mitigation is insufficient.
core_contribution: AGENT-SAFETYBENCH, a benchmark for evaluating the behavioral safety of tool-using LLM agents. It contributes (1) 349 interactive, configurable simulated environments and 2,000 test cases spanning 8 risk categories (250 cases each) and 10 annotated failure modes; (2) a fine-tuned local safety scorer (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) that judges whether an interaction record is safe/unsafe; and (3) an empirical evaluation of 16 LLM agents showing none exceeds a 60% total safety score. Notably the authors' OWN novelty emphasis is on the many environments that lack public APIs (220 of 349 have no similar public-API tools yet), an aspect they argue prior benchmarks overlooked. Cited benchmarks (R-Judge, AgentDojo, ToolEmu, InjecAgent, etc.) are prior work they compare against and partly refine data from, NOT their contribution.
threat_model: Behavior-level (not just content-level) safety risks that arise when an LLM agent interacts with external environments and calls tools. Failures may be unintentional (wrong tool parameters, ignoring constraints, acting on incomplete information) or arise from unsafe user requests / one jailbreak-based content category. The concern is the agent executing harmful actions in its environment (e.g. leaking sensitive data via email, disabling all alarm systems, spreading misinformation via posts/blogs), spanning 8 risk categories: leak sensitive data, property loss, spread unsafe info, physical harm, violate law/ethics, compromise availability, harmful/vulnerable code, and produce unsafe info. The benchmark measures how safely agents behave, not an adversary optimizing attacks (only the 'Produce' category injects jailbreak templates).
limitations: (1) Environments are simulated (JSON schema + Python stubs), not live systems, so real-world execution risks and API behaviors are approximated. (2) The scorer, ground-truth labels, and augmented test cases lean heavily on LLM assistance (GPT-4o, Claude-3.5, Gemini-1.5), risking generator-evaluator correlation and bias; scorer accuracy of 91.5% is measured only on 200 Gemini-1.5-Flash records. (3) Each case is assigned to a single 'most likely' risk category despite possible multi-risk overlap. (4) The 'Produce' content category uses jailbreak templates while behavior categories generally do not, so cross-category score comparisons mix threat setups. (5) Evaluation is a snapshot of late-2024 models (Claude-3.x, GPT-4o, Llama-3.1, Qwen2.5, etc.).

datasets_used: AGENT-SAFETYBENCH (this paper's own: 349 environments, 2,000 test cases, 8 risk categories x 250 cases, 10 failure modes)
R-Judge, AgentDojo, GuardAgent, ToolEmu, ToolSword, InjecAgent (existing datasets refined into 876 cases)
AdvBench (50 raw questions) x 20 jailbreak templates = 200 cases for the 'Produce unsafe information' category
4,000 manually-labeled interaction records (2,186 unsafe / 1,814 safe) from GPT-4o-mini and Claude-3.5-Sonnet, used to train the scorer
quantitative_results: No agent > 60% total safety; average total across 16 agents = 38.5%
Top agents (total safety score): Claude-3-Opus 59.8%, Claude-3.5-Sonnet 59.4%, Claude-3.5-Haiku 55.1%, GPT-4o 44.2%, GPT-4-Turbo 41.9%
Bottom agents: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 18.8%, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct 19.9%, Llama3.1-70B-Instruct 31.2%, GPT-4o-mini 31.2%
Hardest risk category on average: 'Spread unsafe information' 15.6%; easiest: 'Produce' 87.0% (jailbreak-style, well studied)
Lowest-scoring failure modes on average: M7 (ignore known tool issues) 12.5% and M2 (call tools with incomplete info) 18.1%; highest: M1 70.1%, M6 63.3%, M10 63.1%
Fine-tuned Qwen2.5-7B scorer achieves 91.5% binary accuracy on 200 Gemini-1.5-Flash records vs 75.5% for GPT-4o as scorer (~15% improvement claimed over GPT-4o)
Cross-validation: 98% of 200 sampled test cases and 97.5% of 200 sampled safety labels judged reasonable; 94% of GPT-4o-generated explanations judged reasonable
Environment breakdown: 68 similar to existing benchmarks, 42 with public APIs but no sandboxed eval, 220 with no similar public-API tools, 19 with no real-world application yet
baselines_compared: GPT-4o used directly as a safety scorer (75.5% accuracy) - the baseline the fine-tuned scorer beats

16 evaluated LLM agents (systems under test): Claude-3-Opus, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Claude-3.5-Haiku, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4-Turbo, Gemini-1.5-Pro, Gemini-1.5-Flash, DeepSeek-V2.5, GLM4-9B-Chat, Llama3.1-8B/70B/405B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B/14B/72B-Instruct

Prior agent-safety benchmarks compared in Table 1: R-Judge (27 env / 569 cases), AgentDojo (10/267), GuardAgent (2/516), ToolEmu (36/144), ToolSword (6/440), PrivacyLens (6/493), InjecAgent (36/1054), Haicosystem (53/132)
Defense-prompt conditions: simple (list 10 failure modes) vs enhanced (detailed descriptions + examples) prompts, both yielding limited gains
